steps.prompt: Drag the outside point and compare the two tangents
steps.subtitle: From a point outside, you can draw two [tangents](orange) — lines that each just kiss the circle at a single point. Watch the lengths PT₁ and PT₂ in the readout as you drag.

graphic.name: tangent-road
graphic.caption: A wheel resting on a road touches it at a single point. The spoke down to that point meets the road square-on — a perfect right angle.

steps.body:
A [tangent](orange) touches a circle at exactly one point — the point of contact T. This lesson is three facts that all flow from there.
1. The [radius](blue) drawn to T is always perpendicular to the [tangent](orange) — they make a 90° corner. That right angle is the key that unlocks everything else.
2. Where a [tangent](orange) meets a [chord](magenta) at T, the angle between them is half the [arc](teal) the chord cuts off — it behaves just like an inscribed angle in the far "alternate segment".
3. Two [tangents](orange) drawn from the same outside point are equal in length, and the line from that point to the centre cuts the angle between them exactly in half.

steps.prompt: Order the steps to find a tangent–chord angle
steps.subtitle: A [tangent](orange) and a [chord](magenta) meet at the point of contact, and the chord cuts off an [arc](teal) of 100°. Put the method in order.
steps.instruction: Tap the steps into the right order, first move at the top.

items.label: Locate the point of contact, where the tangent meets the chord.
Read off the arc the chord cuts off: 100°.

steps.doneNote: Find the arc, halve it: ½ × 100° = 50°. The tangent–chord angle is half its arc, just like an inscribed angle.

steps.prompt: Why the tangent–chord angle is half the arc

gates.prompt: To tie the angle φ to the circle, which line should we add?

choices.label: The radius OT to the point of contact.
A second tangent from another point.

gates.hint: The radius to the point of contact is the one line that meets the tangent at a right angle.

gates.prompt: Why is triangle OTA isosceles (two equal sides)?

choices.label: OT and OA are both radii, so they are equal.
Because it has a right angle at T.

gates.hint: OT and OA both reach from the centre to the circle, so they are the same length.

gates.prompt: Each base angle is 90° − φ. So the top angle ∠TOA is…

choices.label: 2φ, since all three angles add to 180°.

gates.prompt: ∠TOA = 2φ is a central angle on arc TA, and a central angle equals its arc. So φ is…
gates.hint: arc TA = 2φ, so φ is half the arc.

stages: A tangent touches at T and a chord runs from T to A. Call the tangent–chord angle φ.
Draw the radius OT. The tangent meets the radius at a right angle, so the angle between OT and the chord is 90° − φ.
OT and OA are equal radii, so triangle OTA is isosceles: ∠OAT = ∠OTA = 90° − φ.
The three angles of triangle OTA add to 180°, so the central angle ∠TOA = 2φ — and it stands on arc TA.
A central angle equals its arc, so arc TA = 2φ. Therefore φ = ½ × arc TA — the inscribed angle in the alternate segment.

problems.prompt: A tangent and a chord meet at the point of contact, cutting off a 120° arc. Find the tangent–chord angle.

feedback.correct: Correct. The tangent–chord angle is half the arc: ½ × 120° = 60°.
feedback.incorrect: The tangent–chord angle is half the intercepted arc.

problems.prompt: A tangent–chord angle measures 35°. Find the arc the chord cuts off.

feedback.correct: Correct. The arc is twice the angle: 2 × 35° = 70°.
feedback.incorrect: The arc is twice the tangent–chord angle.

problems.prompt: Two tangents from a point touch a circle, enclosing a 100° arc between the points of contact. Find the angle between the tangents.

feedback.correct: Correct. The angle between the tangents is 180° − 100° = 80°.
feedback.incorrect: The angle between two tangents is 180° minus the near arc between the contact points.

steps.body:
From an outside point, draw one tangent of length PT and one secant meeting the circle at a near point A and a far point B. Then the tangent squared equals the secant's two parts multiplied: PT² = PA × PB.
For example, if PA = 4 and PB = 9, then PT² = 36, so PT = 6.
The tangent is the "in-between" length — the geometric mean of the secant's two parts.

steps.subtitle: One card for each of the three tangent facts — and a few traps.

cards.statement: A tangent meets the radius drawn to the point of contact at 90°.
cards.note: Yes — radius ⊥ tangent. That right angle is the key fact the whole lesson rests on.

cards.statement: A tangent crosses the circle at two points.
cards.note: No — a tangent touches at exactly one point. A line that cuts through at two points is a secant.

cards.statement: The two tangents drawn from a single outside point are equal in length.
cards.note: True — PT₁ = PT₂, as you saw when you dragged the outside point.

cards.statement: A tangent–chord angle equals the whole arc the chord cuts off.
cards.note: No — it is half the arc, just like an inscribed angle. An 80° arc gives a 40° angle, not 80°.

cards.statement: From an outside point, the line to the centre bisects the angle between the two tangents.
cards.note: True — that line splits the angle between the tangents exactly in half.

cards.statement: If two tangents enclose a 100° arc between their contact points, the angle between them is 100°.
cards.note: No — the angle is 180° − 100° = 80°. The angle between two tangents is 180° minus the near arc.

steps.doneNote: Radius ⊥ tangent, tangent–chord = ½ arc, and two tangents from a point are equal — the three facts, locked in.
